text: A media company plans to launch a new global streaming service and wants to ensure they can recover from a regional outage with an RPO of 5 minutes and RTO of 30 minutes for their primary user metadata database. The database has strong consistency and transactional requirements. Which Google Cloud service best meets these business continuity requirements with minimal operational overhead?
options: Cloud SQL for PostgreSQL with cross-region read replicas and manual failover
Bigtable with replication across two regions
Spanner configured as a multi-region instance
Firestore in Native mode with a single regional location
explanation: Spanner multi-region instances provide strong consistency, transactional semantics, and automatic synchronous replication across regions with an RPO of zero and RTO measured in seconds — well within the stated requirements. Cloud SQL replicas are asynchronous (RPO > 0) and require manual failover. Bigtable is non-relational (no transactions across rows). A single-region Firestore deployment fails the regional-outage requirement.

text: Your team is designing a new application that must scale to handle unpredictable bursts of HTTP traffic and scale to zero during quiet periods to optimize cost. The team wants the simplest deployment model. Which compute platform should you recommend?
options: Compute Engine managed instance group with autoscaling based on CPU utilization
GKE Standard cluster with the cluster autoscaler
Cloud Run service
App Engine standard environment with manual scaling
explanation: Cloud Run scales from zero to thousands of instances based on concurrent requests, bills per request and CPU/memory used during request processing, and has the simplest deployment model of the choices. MIGs do not scale to zero. GKE has cluster overhead even when idle. App Engine standard with manual scaling is the opposite of what's required.

text: An on-premises application needs to publish events to a Google Cloud event bus that will be consumed by Cloud Run services. Throughput will be ~50,000 events/second with bursts to 200,000/second. The publisher must use a private network path (no public internet). What is the recommended architecture?
options: Cloud Interconnect or HA VPN to a VPC, Pub/Sub publisher uses Private Service Connect endpoint for the Pub/Sub API, Cloud Run subscribers via push subscriptions
Site-to-Site VPN to a VPC, publisher writes to Cloud Storage objects, Cloud Run reads via Eventarc
Direct Pub/Sub publish over the public internet using API keys
Cloud Interconnect, publisher writes to BigQuery streaming inserts, Dataflow forwards to Cloud Run
explanation: Pub/Sub easily handles the throughput. Private Service Connect creates a private endpoint for the Pub/Sub API inside your VPC, giving the on-prem publisher (reachable via Interconnect or HA VPN) a private network path. Cloud Run push subscriptions are idiomatic for event-driven workloads.

doc_links.title: Private Service Connect for Google APIs

text: You need to share a single VPC across multiple application teams' projects so the network team controls the network while application teams retain ownership of their projects. What is the canonical pattern?
options: VPC Network Peering between each application project's VPC
Network Connectivity Center hub with each project as a spoke
Shared VPC: a host project owns the VPC; service projects attach to it
Place all teams' resources in a single project and separate by labels
explanation: Shared VPC is the purpose-built pattern for centralized network ownership with decentralized application ownership.

text: A retail company is planning to migrate ~600 VMs to Google Cloud over 9 months. They want a discovery + assessment tool that produces TCO analysis, dependency mapping, and migration wave planning before they touch any infrastructure. Which Google Cloud service should be their first stop?
options: Migrate to Virtual Machines
Migration Center
Database Migration Service
Storage Transfer Service
explanation: Migration Center is the unified discovery, assessment, and planning tool: TCO, fits, dependency maps, and wave plans. Migrate to VMs is the *execution* tool used after planning.

text: You need to provide a 99.99% SLA-backed encrypted tunnel between an on-premises data center and a Google Cloud VPC, with dynamic routing. Which configuration meets the requirement?
options: Classic VPN with two tunnels
HA VPN gateway with two tunnels and BGP via Cloud Router
A single VPN tunnel with static routes
Cloud Interconnect Dedicated only — VPN cannot meet 99.99%
explanation: HA VPN provides a 99.99% SLA when configured with two tunnels on different interfaces, paired with BGP via Cloud Router.

text: A data-processing batch job runs nightly for 4 hours, is fault-tolerant, can resume from checkpoints, and you want to minimize compute cost. What configuration should you choose for the worker fleet?
options: On-demand N2 standard VMs in a regional MIG
Spot VMs in a regional MIG, with checkpointing to GCS
Cloud Functions invocations chained with Workflows
Reserved 3-year committed-use VMs
explanation: Spot VMs are typically 60-91% cheaper than on-demand and ideal for fault-tolerant batch with checkpoints.

text: Your security team requires that storage buckets containing customer PII can only be accessed by workloads inside specific VPC networks, even if a leaked service-account key were used externally. Which control enforces this?
options: Customer-managed encryption keys (CMEK) on the buckets
IAM Conditions restricting access by request.time
VPC Service Controls perimeter that includes the storage project
Uniform bucket-level access with public access prevention
explanation: VPC Service Controls create a network-aware service perimeter: API calls to protected services from outside the perimeter are rejected even with valid credentials.

text: A workload running on AWS EC2 needs to read objects from a Google Cloud Storage bucket. The security team forbids creating long-lived service-account keys. What is the recommended approach?
options: Generate a JSON service account key, store it in AWS Secrets Manager, mount it on EC2, and rotate it weekly with a Lambda function
Configure Workload Identity Federation with AWS as the IdP so the EC2 IAM role can impersonate a Google Cloud service account
Use a long-lived service account key delivered via EC2 user-data, restricted by IAM Conditions and bucket-level public access prevention
Generate short-lived OAuth 2.0 user credentials via gcloud auth application-default login and cache them in EC2 instance metadata
explanation: Workload Identity Federation lets external identities (AWS IAM role, in this case) exchange their native tokens for short-lived Google Cloud credentials, removing the need for any long-lived SA key. Option A still produces a long-lived key (rotation weekly is still 'long-lived' by security standards). Option C uses a long-lived key — IAM Conditions don't change its lifetime. Option D requires interactive user credentials, which don't exist on a server.

text: You want to restrict all projects in your organization so that Compute Engine VMs cannot be created with external IP addresses. What is the most appropriate mechanism?
options: A custom Cloud Asset Inventory feed plus a Cloud Function that detects new VMs with external IPs and deletes the NIC's access config
An organization policy with constraint constraints/compute.vmExternalIpAccess set to deny all, applied at the org with allowlist exceptions per folder
An IAM deny policy at the org node that denies compute.instances.setMachineType to all principals
A VPC Service Controls perimeter around the Compute Engine API plus an org-wide hierarchical firewall denying all egress to 0.0.0.0/0
explanation: constraints/compute.vmExternalIpAccess is the purpose-built org policy constraint that *prevents* external IPs at create time, enforceable at org/folder/project with allowlists. Detect-and-remediate (option A) leaves a window where the IP exists. Option C denies the wrong permission and breaks legitimate use. VPC-SC + egress-deny (D) doesn't stop external IP attachment and breaks all outbound traffic.

text: A healthcare provider is deploying an EHR application on Google Cloud and must comply with HIPAA. Which combination provides the strongest defense-in-depth?
options: Sign a BAA with Google Cloud, use HIPAA-eligible services only, enable Cloud Audit Logs Data Access, encrypt with CMEK, and apply VPC Service Controls
Use only public preview services since they update fastest
Store PHI in Cloud Storage with public ACLs and rely on long random object names
Skip the BAA — Google Cloud is HIPAA-compliant by default
explanation: HIPAA on GCP requires a signed BAA, use of HIPAA-eligible services, and best practices include enabling Data Access audit logs, CMEK, and VPC-SC.

text: Your team wants a managed API platform supporting versioning, OAuth/JWT enforcement, rate limiting per developer, monetization, and a developer portal. Which Google Cloud service is the right fit?
options: API Gateway
Apigee
Cloud Endpoints with ESPv2
Cloud Load Balancing with URL maps
explanation: Apigee is the full-lifecycle API management platform: versioning, advanced policies, monetization, developer portals.

text: Per the Google Cloud Architecture Framework's operational excellence pillar, which practice should be foundational from day one of a new service?
options: Add observability (logs, metrics, traces, SLOs) so the team can detect and diagnose issues — instrument before launch
Optimize cost above all else; observability can be added later
Deploy manually to production until traffic patterns stabilize
Use only Google-managed services so observability is unnecessary
explanation: The framework emphasizes 'observe everything' as a day-one operational excellence principle.

text: A regulated EU customer requires that all data for their workload be stored and processed only within the EU, and that Google personnel cannot access the customer's data without explicit, audited approval per access. Which combination of controls best meets this requirement?
options: Sign a BAA, use multi-region 'global' resources, enable Cloud Audit Logs
Pin resources to EU regions only, enable Assured Workloads for EU Regions and Support, enable Access Approval, and use CMEK with EKM keys
Use only Cloud Storage with bucket-lock; data residency is automatic
Configure VPC Service Controls and rely on encryption at rest
explanation: Assured Workloads enforces region pinning and personnel/support residency. Access Approval requires explicit customer approval (with reason logged) before any Google access. CMEK with EKM keeps the key under customer control.

text: A team needs a managed Kubernetes platform but doesn't want to manage node pools, upgrades, autoscaling, or worry about per-node sizing. They have variable, unpredictable workloads and want to pay only for pod requests. Which option fits best?
options: GKE Standard with cluster autoscaler
GKE Autopilot
Cloud Run for Anthos
GKE on VMware (Anthos clusters on bare metal)
explanation: GKE Autopilot manages the entire node infrastructure, charges per pod resource requests, and is Google's recommended default for new GKE workloads.

text: A globally accessible HTTP application requires SSL termination, path-based routing, integration with Cloud CDN, and protection by Cloud Armor with WAF rules. Which load balancer should you select?
options: Regional external Network Load Balancer (passthrough)
Global external Application Load Balancer
Regional internal Application Load Balancer
Cross-region internal Application Load Balancer
explanation: Only the global external Application Load Balancer integrates with Cloud CDN and supports global anycast IPs, path-based URL maps, SSL termination, and Cloud Armor WAF.

text: Your engineering org uses many GCP projects organized under folders by business unit. You want a base set of guardrails (org policies, audit log sinks, billing alerts) that every new folder/project inherits, with the ability to layer team-specific exceptions. What is the right primitive?
options: Apply the guardrails as IAM allow/deny policies attached to each project's default Compute Engine service account at provisioning time
Use the Resource Hierarchy: set base org policies and aggregated log/billing sinks at the Organization node, fine-tune at folders, and allow exceptions at projects
Maintain a runbook the platform team applies manually after each new project is created, with quarterly drift reviews via Cloud Asset Inventory
Use Cloud Asset Inventory to detect drift after the fact and trigger a Cloud Function to reconcile each project back to a baseline JSON in Cloud Storage
explanation: The Resource Hierarchy (Org → Folders → Projects) is precisely designed for inherited policy with overrides at any lower level. IAM on a default SA cannot enforce org-wide constraints like external-IP denial or log routing. Manual runbooks do not scale and create drift. Reconciling-after-the-fact leaves a noncompliance window and reinvents Org Policy poorly.

text: A latency-sensitive analytics workload reads ~10 TB/day of immutable Parquet files written by an ingest pipeline. The team queries with Spark on Dataproc Serverless from one region, but other teams in different regions also need occasional reads. They want low cost and predictable latency for the primary region while still allowing cross-region access without manually copying data. Which storage configuration fits best?
options: Multi-region Standard bucket co-located with the primary region
Dual-region Standard bucket with the primary region as one of the two regions, using turbo replication
Regional Coldline bucket in the primary region only
Regional Standard bucket in the primary region; teams in other regions transfer copies via Storage Transfer Service
explanation: Dual-region buckets give regional-class performance from each of the two regions, optional turbo replication for tight RPO, and a single bucket all teams can read from.

text: You want secrets (DB passwords, API keys) to be centrally stored, automatically rotated where supported, version-controlled, and accessible to Cloud Run via IAM-controlled environment variables. What's the right service?
options: Cloud KMS
Secret Manager
GCS bucket with object-level IAM
Cloud Asset Inventory
explanation: Secret Manager is purpose-built for secrets: versioning, IAM, rotation, automatic mounting into Cloud Run as env vars or files.

text: You need to ingest streaming clickstream events at ~1M events/sec, run windowed aggregations and fraud-detection joins in flight, and land both raw and enriched events in BigQuery. What's the recommended GCP-native pipeline?
options: Pub/Sub → Dataflow streaming (Apache Beam) → BigQuery
Cloud Storage → Dataproc batch → BigQuery
Cloud Functions → BigQuery streaming inserts directly
Pub/Sub → Cloud Run with manual sharding → BigQuery
explanation: Pub/Sub + Dataflow is the canonical streaming ETL pattern on GCP. Dataflow handles windowing, watermarks, and exactly-once semantics into BigQuery.

text: Your analytics team wants ad-hoc SQL over petabytes with no infrastructure to manage, plus the ability to query data in Cloud Storage without ingesting it. Which feature pair fits?
options: BigQuery + BigLake external tables
Cloud SQL + read replicas
Bigtable + Dataflow
Spanner + federated queries
explanation: BigQuery is the serverless analytics engine; BigLake external tables expose Parquet/ORC/Avro in GCS as governed BigQuery tables without ingestion.

text: You're modernizing a monolithic Java app. The team wants to break it into services incrementally. Which 'strangler fig' pattern works on GCP with minimal new infrastructure for routing?
options: Put a global external Application Load Balancer in front; create URL map rules that route specific paths to new Cloud Run services while the rest still points to the legacy backend
Rewrite everything before deploying any of it
Deploy both versions to different VPCs with no shared ingress
Use DNS round-robin between old and new
explanation: URL-map-based routing on the global ALB is the classic strangler-fig enabler: peel off endpoints one path at a time without changing client URLs.

text: Per Google's Architecture Framework, which is the preferred ordering when an incident is in progress?
options: Diagnose → Mitigate → Communicate
Mitigate → Communicate → Diagnose
Communicate → Diagnose → Mitigate
Wait → Escalate → Diagnose
explanation: SRE practice is mitigate first (stop user pain), keep stakeholders informed, then diagnose. This is also the framework's recommended incident-response sequencing.

text: Your organization wants centralized control of network policies and shared subnets across many application projects. The networking team owns the network; application teams own their workloads. Which design matches Google's recommended foundation?
options: Give every application project its own VPC and connect them with VPC Network Peering through a central transit project
A host project with the Shared VPC plus folder-level hierarchical firewall policies; service projects attach for each application team
Place all teams in a single project, separate workloads by labels, and use IAM Conditions on resource.labels for tenant isolation
One VPC per application project plus a single Cloud Router instance shared across them via NCC hub-and-spoke
explanation: Shared VPC + folder-level hierarchical firewall policies is the security-foundations blueprint pattern. VPC Peering scales poorly (n*n) and doesn't centralize firewall rules. Single project violates blast-radius isolation and lets one team's IAM mistake hit everyone. NCC hub-and-spoke is for cross-VPC/on-prem connectivity, not for centralized firewall policy across application teams.

text: You manage GCP infrastructure with Terraform from a CI pipeline. State is stored in a GCS bucket. Which configuration improves resilience against accidental loss of state?
options: Disable bucket versioning to keep state files small
Enable bucket versioning + uniform bucket-level access + lifecycle rules to retain N old versions
Store state in the same project as the workloads
Use local backend on the CI runner and upload to GCS at the end
explanation: Versioning + UBLA + a retention lifecycle is the standard hardening for TF state buckets.

text: You manage many GKE clusters across an enterprise and want consistent policies, multi-cluster service discovery, and centralized config management. Which feature should you adopt?
options: GKE fleets with Config Sync and Policy Controller
A single mega-cluster spanning all teams
Manual kubectl apply from a jumphost
ArgoCD instances per cluster with no central control plane
explanation: GKE fleets are the unit of multi-cluster management; Config Sync (GitOps) and Policy Controller (OPA Gatekeeper) deliver centralized config and policy.

text: You need to roll out a new Cloud Run revision to 10% of traffic, watch SLO health for 30 minutes, then progress to 100%. Which Google Cloud service orchestrates this canary release?
options: Cloud Build with a custom shell step calling gcloud run services update-traffic on a timer between phases
A Cloud Deploy delivery pipeline configured with a canary strategy and a verify phase that queries the SLO
Cloud Run traffic splitting set manually in the console between revisions, with operators watching SLOs by hand
GKE rolling updates with PodDisruptionBudgets, fronted by a global Application Load Balancer pointing at the Cloud Run service
explanation: Cloud Deploy is the release orchestration product; canary strategy declaratively defines phases (e.g. 10% → 100%) with verify jobs between them. Cloud Build can drive deploys but isn't a release orchestrator with native canary phases. Manual traffic splitting + manual watching has no automation or audit trail. Rolling updates apply to GKE, not Cloud Run, and an LB in front of Cloud Run doesn't change the deployment unit.

text: Your security policy requires that only container images signed by your CI's attestor can be deployed to GKE production clusters. Which Google Cloud service enforces this at admission time?
options: Artifact Registry vulnerability scanning
Binary Authorization with an attestor and a cluster policy that requires the attestation
Cloud Armor with WAF rules
VPC Service Controls
explanation: Binary Authorization is the admission controller that enforces signed-image requirements at deploy time on GKE and Cloud Run.

text: Which framework defines hierarchical assurance levels for software supply chain integrity, and which Google Cloud product helps you produce attestations to satisfy higher levels?
options: SLSA framework; Cloud Build emits SLSA build provenance and Binary Authorization gates deploy on attestors signed against that provenance
SOC 2 framework; Security Command Center collects evidence and emits attestations for deployments via a Cloud Run admission webhook
PCI DSS framework; Cloud Armor's adaptive protection emits per-build attestations consumed by GKE Policy Controller
ISO 27001 framework; Cloud KMS signs each container image at build time and the signature is verified by Artifact Registry on pull
explanation: SLSA is the supply-chain integrity framework; Cloud Build emits SLSA-compliant build provenance, and Binary Authorization attestors gate deployment on it. SOC 2/PCI DSS/ISO 27001 are general assurance/compliance frameworks that don't define supply-chain integrity levels, and the products listed (SCC, Cloud Armor, KMS) don't produce build provenance.

text: A team's SLO is 99.9% availability over 28 days. They observe a sustained burn rate of 6× and want an alert that fires fast enough to act before the budget is exhausted, but not so noisily that minor blips page on-call. What's the recommended approach?
options: Page on every error
Multi-window, multi-burn-rate alerting (e.g. fast-burn 14.4× over 1h AND slow-burn 6× over 6h)
Alert only on weekly budget review
Disable alerts and check the dashboard each morning
explanation: Multi-burn-rate alerting reduces false positives while still meeting time-to-detect goals. Cloud Monitoring SLOs implement this directly.

text: You need to provision GPU capacity for a 6-hour training job at a specific time tomorrow, in a region where on-demand GPUs are frequently unavailable. Which approach is most appropriate?
options: Have a Cloud Scheduler job retry on-demand provisioning every minute until the GPU request succeeds in the target region
Use Dynamic Workload Scheduler in calendar mode to request a reservation for the required GPUs at the desired start time
Provision a 1-year committed-use discount for the GPU SKU to guarantee capacity in that region for the one-time job
Rewrite the job to run on Spot GPU VMs with no checkpointing and accept restarts on preemption, since Spot quota is usually available
explanation: Dynamic Workload Scheduler is purpose-built for time-bound AI/HPC capacity — calendar mode for known windows, flex-start for queued jobs. Polling for on-demand wastes time and isn't a capacity guarantee. A 1-year CUD over-commits for a single 6-hour job. Spot without checkpoints will lose all progress on every preemption.

text: An incident is in progress: a new release is causing elevated 5xx errors. The fastest mitigation that respects the SRE principle 'mitigate before diagnose' is:
options: Open a debugging session and grep logs
Roll back the release to the previous known-good revision and then investigate
Email all stakeholders and wait for an architect to weigh in
Disable monitoring to silence the alert
explanation: SRE practice prioritizes user-visible mitigation (rollback / drain / redirect / capacity) before root-cause analysis.

text: You operate a hybrid environment: GKE on Google Cloud and Kubernetes clusters on-prem. You want a single, OpenTelemetry-compatible metrics backend with Prometheus query language. Which option fits?
options: Run a separate Prometheus + Grafana stack per cluster, manually federated
Google Cloud Managed Service for Prometheus, with collectors running in each cluster
Cloud Logging only — metrics aren't needed
Send all metrics to BigQuery via Pub/Sub
explanation: Managed Service for Prometheus is the GCP-native, PromQL-compatible metrics backend designed for hybrid environments.

text: Your Cloud Logging bill has grown sharply. Investigation shows that ~80% of ingestion is from one verbose application's INFO logs, which are not used for alerting or audit. What is the most cost-effective mitigation?
options: Delete the logs after ingestion using a TTL policy
Add an exclusion filter in the _Default sink to drop those INFO logs before ingest
Switch the application to write logs to Cloud Storage manually
Move all workloads to a different region
explanation: Logging is billed primarily on ingestion volume. An exclusion filter on the _Default sink drops matching logs before the ingest meter runs.

text: Which Cloud Audit Logs category is enabled by default and free, vs. one that is off by default and incurs ingestion charges when enabled?
options: Admin Activity (default-on, free) vs. Data Access (default-off, billed)
Both are default-on and free
Both are default-off and billed
System Event (default-off) vs. Policy Denied (default-on)
explanation: Admin Activity audit logs are always enabled and free. Data Access audit logs are off by default and incur Logging ingestion costs when enabled.

text: You want a dashboard panel that shows p95 latency of a service grouped by route, using PromQL. Which combination supports this in Cloud Monitoring?
options: Use one of the built-in service dashboards — Cloud Monitoring panels accept PromQL only when paired with Cloud Trace as the metric source
Build a custom dashboard with a PromQL widget querying Managed Service for Prometheus metrics scraped from the service's /metrics endpoint
Export the metrics to Looker Studio first — native Cloud Monitoring dashboards only support MQL and the legacy filter UI, not PromQL
Use Cloud Profiler to compute p95 latency per route — Cloud Monitoring dashboards don't support latency histograms or PromQL syntax
explanation: Cloud Monitoring dashboards support PromQL widgets natively, especially when paired with Managed Service for Prometheus. PromQL is not gated on Cloud Trace, MQL is not the only supported language, and Cloud Profiler is for CPU/heap profiling — not latency dashboards.

text: Your stateless web tier on Compute Engine has steady 24/7 traffic. The team wants to maximize discount without commitment-management overhead. What applies automatically?
options: Spot VM pricing
Sustained-Use Discounts, applied automatically based on per-month usage
1-year committed-use discount, requires manual purchase
Free tier
explanation: Sustained-Use Discounts (SUDs) apply automatically to GCE/GKE for resources running a large fraction of the month, with no commitment.

text: Your CI workflow needs to authenticate to Google Cloud from GitHub Actions without storing a long-lived service-account key as a GitHub secret. What is the recommended pattern?
options: Use a JSON service-account key stored in a GitHub encrypted secret
Use Workload Identity Federation: GitHub OIDC token → Google Cloud short-lived credential via google-github-actions/auth
Embed a refresh token in the workflow file
Use gcloud user credentials interactively
explanation: Workload Identity Federation is the keyless pattern recommended by Google and used by this repo's bootstrap.

text: You're rolling out a backwards-incompatible API change. Which deployment pattern lets you have both old and new versions running side-by-side and shift users gradually with the ability to cut back instantly?
options: Recreate strategy
Blue/green deployment with a router/load-balancer-controlled cutover
In-place update
Big-bang deploy
explanation: Blue/green keeps both stacks fully provisioned. The cutover is a router config change, and rollback is the inverse change — instant.

text: Your team wants integrated, opinionated CI/CD on GCP that builds container images, runs tests, scans them for vulnerabilities, and stores artifacts ready for Cloud Deploy to roll out. Which pair fits best?
options: Cloud Build (CI: build/test/scan/push) + Artifact Registry (storage) + Cloud Deploy (CD)
Cloud Functions only
GKE custom Tekton pipeline + Container Registry
Cloud Workstations
explanation: Cloud Build → Artifact Registry → Cloud Deploy is the canonical native trio. Container Registry is deprecated.

doc_links.title: Cloud Build + AR + Cloud Deploy reference

text: What is an SLI vs SLO vs SLA in SRE practice?
options: SLI = the measurement (e.g. % requests <300ms); SLO = the target (e.g. 99.9% over 28d); SLA = the contractual obligation with consequences
All three are interchangeable terms
SLI is for internal teams only and never measured
SLO is for customers; SLI is for engineers; SLA is for executives only
explanation: SLI = indicator; SLO = internal target; SLA = externally promised number with consequences. SLOs are typically tighter than SLAs.

text: After a Sev-1 incident, what is the SRE-recommended best practice for postmortems?
options: Identify the engineer responsible and discipline them
Write a blameless postmortem focused on systemic causes and concrete action items, share it widely
Skip the postmortem if the issue is already fixed
Keep the postmortem confidential to a small executive group
explanation: Blameless postmortems focus on the system, not individuals, producing actionable improvements.

text: You need to debug a latency issue that spans Cloud Run, Pub/Sub, and Cloud SQL. Which Google Cloud product correlates spans across all three so you can see end-to-end timing?
options: Cloud Profiler
Cloud Trace (with OpenTelemetry instrumentation)
Cloud Logging only
Error Reporting
explanation: Cloud Trace receives OpenTelemetry-formatted spans from your services and stitches them into end-to-end traces.

text: GKE pods are being OOMKilled in production but not in staging. The team's release process needs an automated guard before promoting to prod. Which control would catch this most directly?
options: Add a Cloud Deploy verify job that runs a representative load test against the staging Service and asserts container memory_working_set_bytes stays under the configured pod memory limit before promotion
Increase the staging cluster's node pool to use larger machine types so staging memory headroom matches production exactly
Disable the Vertical Pod Autoscaler in production to keep pod requests stable and prevent OOMKill thrashing from misconfigured recommendations
Add a HorizontalPodAutoscaler scaling on memory utilization with a target of 70% so prod automatically scales out under memory pressure
explanation: A Cloud Deploy verify job that load-tests against the staging working-set vs. configured pod limit catches the misconfiguration *before* promotion. Bigger staging nodes mask the problem (the pod limit is the constraint, not node capacity). Disabling VPA does nothing about an under-sized memory limit. HPA on memory adds replicas but each replica still has the same too-small limit — they all OOM.

text: Which Google Cloud feature lets you set spending thresholds with email alerts (and optional Pub/Sub notifications) per project or billing account, without capping spend automatically?
options: Quotas
Budgets and budget alerts in Cloud Billing
Cloud Armor rate limits
Recommender API
explanation: Budgets emit alerts at configurable percentages of a target; pairing with Pub/Sub allows automated reactions. Quotas cap usage but don't track spend.

text: A platform team wants to provide self-service infrastructure for application teams via a curated catalog (e.g. 'GKE cluster', 'Cloud SQL instance') with guardrails enforced. Which approach aligns with Google's platform-engineering recommendation?
options: Give application teams Owner role on their own projects and let them write Terraform from scratch
Build a service catalog using Service Catalog and/or Config Controller with curated Terraform/Config Connector blueprints
Email the platform team to provision each resource manually
Use only Cloud Console click-ops
explanation: Curated, opinionated blueprints exposed via Service Catalog or Config Controller (KRM/Config Connector) is the platform-engineering pattern Google recommends.

text: A small SaaS startup wants to deploy a stateless web tier across multiple zones in a single region so a zonal outage doesn't take down the service. The team uses Compute Engine VMs. Which option achieves this with the least operational overhead?
options: A single large VM with hourly snapshots restored manually after a failure
A regional managed instance group (MIG) with an instance template, fronted by a regional load balancer
Three independent VMs in three regions with DNS round-robin
AWS Auto Scaling Groups peered into the GCP VPC
explanation: A regional MIG spreads instances across zones automatically, recreates failed VMs, and integrates with load balancing — the canonical zonal-HA pattern. A single VM with snapshots cannot meet zonal HA. Multi-region DNS round-robin is overkill (and inconsistent) for a zonal-failure requirement. AWS ASGs are not a GCP service.

text: A financial-services workload must run on Google Cloud with these constraints: (1) one component requires privileged init containers to tune kernel networking parameters; (2) it needs reliable access to NVIDIA GPUs; (3) the team is willing to use Spot capacity but must receive preemption signals to drain in-flight work; (4) they want to avoid managing individual VMs and OS patching. Which compute platform is the BEST fit?
options: Cloud Run (2nd-gen execution environment) with GPU support and concurrency tuning
GKE Standard with a dedicated GPU node pool using Spot VMs and a node-termination handler
GKE Autopilot with GPU Spot Pods
Compute Engine Spot VMs with a custom container runtime managed by the team
explanation: GKE Standard is the only option that satisfies ALL four constraints: privileged init containers are allowed, GPU node pools are first-class, Spot VMs surface a preemption notice, and Google manages the control plane while node OS patching is largely automated via auto-upgrade. Autopilot disallows privileged containers. Cloud Run does not support privileged init containers and has stricter GPU/runtime constraints. Plain Compute Engine forces the team to own the runtime and patching.

text: An on-prem MySQL 8.0 database must be migrated to Cloud SQL for MySQL with minimal downtime. The team needs to validate the target for several days while the source is still live, then cut over during a planned window. Which Google Cloud service supports this with continuous change capture?
options: Storage Transfer Service with a recurring schedule reading mysqldump output written to Cloud Storage by an on-prem cron job
Database Migration Service (DMS) using continuous (CDC) migration mode with promotion at the planned cutover window
Cloud Composer DAG that runs mysqldump every 5 minutes and re-imports the full dataset into Cloud SQL using a Python operator
BigQuery Data Transfer Service for MySQL with a CDC-style scheduled pull from the source binary log into BigQuery, then export to Cloud SQL
explanation: DMS performs an initial dump and then streams binlog changes from the source, keeping Cloud SQL in near-sync until promotion — the standard low-downtime MySQL/PostgreSQL pattern. Storage Transfer Service moves objects, not relational change streams. Repeated full mysqldumps cannot meet the downtime/validation goal and overwhelm the source. BigQuery DTS targets analytics sources and does not provide a Cloud SQL CDC path.

text: After completing assessment in Migration Center, your team is ready to lift-and-shift ~200 VMware VMs to Google Cloud with the smallest possible application changes and the ability to test cutover before final switch. Which execution tool is the right fit?
options: Migrate to Virtual Machines (m2VM) with replication and test clones
Database Migration Service
Storage Transfer Service
Manually rebuild each VM with Cloud Build templates
explanation: Migrate to Virtual Machines is purpose-built for VM lift-and-shift: it replicates source disks, lets you launch test-clone VMs in GCE, and supports a controlled cutover. DMS is for databases; STS is for objects; manual rebuilds defeat lift-and-shift.

text: Where in the Google Cloud resource hierarchy should you set an organization policy that you want every project to inherit by default?
options: On each project individually after it is created
At the Organization node, with optional folder/project overrides
On the billing account
In a Cloud Storage bucket labeled 'policies'
explanation: Org policies set at the Organization node are inherited by all folders and projects below; folders/projects can override where allowed. Per-project setup defeats inheritance and is error-prone.

text: An enterprise needs a private Layer-2 connection to Google Cloud with sustained 100 Gbps capacity and no traffic over the public internet. Which product fits?
options: HA VPN with two tunnels
Dedicated Interconnect
Cloud NAT with a reserved external IP
Azure ExpressRoute
explanation: Dedicated Interconnect provides 10/100 Gbps physical links into Google's network with no internet traversal. HA VPN runs over the public internet and is bandwidth-limited per tunnel (~3 Gbps). Cloud NAT only translates outbound VM traffic. ExpressRoute is a Microsoft Azure product.

text: A global e-commerce platform runs Cloud Run services in three regions. Requirements: (1) a single anycast IPv4 published in DNS; (2) automatic failover away from an unhealthy region; (3) Cloud CDN for static assets at /assets/*; (4) path-based routing so /api/* hits the application backend; (5) cookie-based session affinity on /api/*. Which architecture BEST satisfies all five?
options: GeoDNS with three regional external Application Load Balancers, each with its own external IP
A global external Application Load Balancer with a URL map: /assets/* → Cloud CDN-enabled backend bucket; /api/* → serverless NEG backends across regions with generated-cookie session affinity
Regional internal Application Load Balancers behind a public Cloud DNS record
Global external TCP Proxy Load Balancer with serverless NEGs and Cloud CDN
explanation: Only the global external ALB delivers a single anycast IP, multi-region serverless NEGs with automatic regional failover, URL-map-based path routing, Cloud CDN integration on a backend bucket, and cookie-based session affinity on a backend service. GeoDNS requires multiple IPs and DNS-cache-bound failover. Internal ALBs cannot serve public traffic. TCP Proxy LB is L4 only — no URL maps, no Cloud CDN.

text: A weekly ML training job runs 12–18 hours on GPU VMs and checkpoints to GCS every 30 minutes. The team wants the lowest cost while accepting that a preemption may cost up to one checkpoint interval of recomputation. Which choice is best?
options: On-demand A2 GPU VMs in a regional MIG
Spot GPU VMs with a startup script that resumes from the latest GCS checkpoint
1-year committed-use discount on enough A2 capacity to run 24/7
Cloud Run jobs (because they autoscale to zero)
explanation: Spot GPUs are 60–91% cheaper and the checkpointing already bounds the cost of a preemption. A 1-year CUD pays for full-time capacity even though the job uses ~6% of the week. Cloud Run jobs have a 60-minute task limit, far short of 12–18 hours.

text: Compliance requires retaining contract PDFs for 7 years. They are read at most once per year (audits) and write-once. Which Cloud Storage class minimizes cost?
options: Standard
Nearline
Coldline
Archive
explanation: Archive is designed for data accessed less than once per year and offers the lowest storage price; minimum storage duration (365 days) easily fits a 7-year retention.

text: A regulated customer requires that the encryption keys protecting their Cloud Storage data NEVER reside inside Google's infrastructure, and that Google must call out to the customer's key system for every cryptographic operation — so that revoking access at the customer side immediately blocks Google from decrypting. Which option meets this?
options: Google-managed encryption keys (default)
CMEK with keys in Cloud KMS software protection level
CMEK with Cloud HSM-backed keys
CMEK with Cloud External Key Manager (Cloud EKM) referencing keys held in the customer's external KMS/HSM
explanation: Cloud EKM keeps the key material in the customer's external KMS/HSM; Google must reach out for every wrap/unwrap, and revocation at the external KMS immediately denies Google. CMEK (software or HSM) still stores the key inside Google. Default Google-managed keys are entirely Google-controlled.

text: A team wants to detect misconfigurations (publicly exposed buckets, over-privileged service accounts, open firewall rules) and high-severity vulnerabilities across all projects, with findings centralized for the security team. Which Google Cloud product is the right hub?
options: Cloud Asset Inventory with scheduled exports to a BigQuery dataset and custom SQL detectors written by the security team
Security Command Center with Security Health Analytics and Web Security Scanner enabled
Cloud Logging with org-level log-based metrics and a custom alerting policy per misconfiguration class
Cloud Armor security policies plus Recommender API recommendations triaged in a per-project ticket queue
explanation: Security Command Center is the centralized security and risk management hub; Security Health Analytics is the built-in detector for the listed misconfigurations and Web Security Scanner finds web vulns. Asset Inventory is raw resource state — you'd be rebuilding SCC from scratch. Log-based metrics can't see most of the listed posture issues. Cloud Armor protects HTTP backends, not posture.

text: You need to rotate a database password stored in Secret Manager every 30 days, with no human in the loop. Which design is idiomatic on GCP?
options: Enable Cloud KMS automatic rotation on the key encrypting the secret; configure the rotation period to 30 days so the underlying ciphertext is re-wrapped
Configure Secret Manager rotation: a Pub/Sub topic on a 30-day schedule triggers a Cloud Function that mints a new password, updates the database, and adds a new secret version
Schedule a Cloud Scheduler job calling a custom Cloud Run service that overwrites the same secret version in place every 30 days using addSecretVersion with deletion of older versions
Configure a Cloud Composer DAG that runs monthly, generates a new password using a Python operator, writes it to a versioned object in Cloud Storage, and updates the DB password from there
explanation: Secret Manager's built-in rotation publishes to a Pub/Sub topic on the configured cadence; the subscriber (typically a Cloud Function) rotates the credential at the source DB and adds a new secret version. KMS key rotation rotates the wrapping key, not the secret value (the password is unchanged). Overwriting versions in place defeats versioning and rollback. A Composer DAG dropping passwords in GCS objects bypasses Secret Manager entirely and is a security regression.

text: Your analyst team wants to run nightly SQL transformations on data already in BigQuery, with no servers, no orchestrator, and the simplest possible setup. Which feature fits?
options: Dataproc cluster running Spark SQL
BigQuery scheduled queries
Dataflow batch pipeline
Cloud Composer DAG with a BashOperator
explanation: Scheduled queries run a SQL statement on a cron schedule directly inside BigQuery — no infrastructure to manage. Dataproc/Dataflow/Composer are all heavier than required for in-warehouse SQL transforms.

text: A 50 TB BigQuery sales fact table is queried hundreds of times per day. Almost every query filters by sale_date (last 90 days) and groups/filters by region. Costs are spiking and p95 latency is climbing. Which combination most directly addresses BOTH problems?
options: Add a covering BigQuery materialized view per analyst
Partition by sale_date and cluster by region; purchase a slot reservation (BI Engine + edition) to cap and predict cost
Move the table to Bigtable for faster scans
Export the table to Parquet in GCS and query via BigLake only
explanation: Date partitioning prunes 90 days vs. full-scan; clustering on region further prunes within partitions — directly cutting bytes scanned and latency. Slot reservations make spend predictable. Per-analyst materialized views proliferate cost. Bigtable is a NoSQL key-value store, not a SQL analytics engine.

text: You're modernizing a monolith into microservices on GKE. Internal services need L7 features (gRPC, retries, circuit breaking) and mTLS between pods, with no external exposure. Which solution best fits?
options: Global external Application Load Balancer in front of every microservice
Cloud Service Mesh (managed Istio) providing mTLS, retries, and L7 traffic management between services
Regional external Network Load Balancer per service
Cloud NAT with custom routing
explanation: Cloud Service Mesh delivers pod-to-pod mTLS and L7 traffic management (gRPC, retries, circuit breakers) without exposing services externally. External ALBs/NLBs would expose internal services and don't provide mTLS between pods.

text: A team wants to track an availability SLO for a Cloud Run API and be alerted when error budget is being burned aggressively, while suppressing noise from short blips. Which approach should they implement?
options: Add a metric-threshold alerting policy on the Cloud Run 5xx-rate metric with a 5-minute alignment window and the default trigger condition of 'any time series violates'
Define a request-based SLO in Cloud Monitoring and add a multi-burn-rate policy (fast-burn 14.4× over 1h AND slow-burn 6× over 6h)
Configure a single low-burn-rate (1×) alerting condition over a 24-hour window so the team is paged once daily summarizing the day's error budget consumption
Build a custom Cloud Function on a Cloud Scheduler trigger that queries the Monitoring API every hour and pages on-call if 5xx > 100 in the prior hour
explanation: Cloud Monitoring SLOs natively compute burn rate; the multi-window/multi-burn-rate pattern from the SRE Workbook catches severe incidents fast while suppressing blips. A static 5xx threshold has no SLO context — it pages on every spike. A 1× single-window policy detects burns far too late (and a daily summary isn't an alert). A custom Cloud Function reinvents the SLO machinery poorly.

text: A fintech runs three workload classes on GCP: (1) an order-processing API on GKE that runs 24/7 and cannot be interrupted; (2) a nightly ML training job that checkpoints every 15 minutes and tolerates restarts; (3) on-demand analytics export jobs lasting <30 minutes that are fully fault-tolerant. Which cost strategy BEST matches each workload independently?
options: 1-year CUD for all three workloads
1-year CUD for the order API; Spot VMs for the ML training job; Spot VMs for the export jobs
Spot VMs for all three workloads
On-demand for all three; rely on sustained-use discounts only
explanation: CUDs reward 24/7 steady-state usage and protect the non-interruptible API. Spot is ideal for both fault-tolerant workloads (ML with checkpoints, short-lived exports). Putting Spot under the order API risks outages; CUDs for spiky/short jobs strands commitment; SUD-only leaves money on the table for the steady workload.

text: An enterprise migrating ~300 projects to GCP needs: (a) per-business-unit chargeback; (b) org-wide security guardrails with BU-level customization; (c) centralized network management; (d) blast-radius isolation per BU; (e) self-service project provisioning that automatically inherits guardrails. Which foundation BEST satisfies ALL five?
options: Flat list of projects under the org node, IAM grants per project

Folder hierarchy (Bootstrap, Common, BU folders → env folders → projects), Shared VPC host project(s), org policies at the org with folder overrides, separate billing sub-accounts per BU, a Project Factory (Terraform) for self-service
One mega-project per BU with everything separated by labels
A separate GCP Organization per BU
explanation: This is the security-foundations blueprint: folders enable inherited org policies and BU isolation; Shared VPC centralizes networking; per-BU billing sub-accounts enable chargeback; a Project Factory delivers self-service with guardrails baked in. Flat projects defeat inheritance/scale; one project per BU has no isolation; separate Orgs fragment governance.

text: An engineer added a firewall rule via the Cloud Console, bypassing your Terraform pipeline. What is the most appropriate way to detect AND reconcile this drift going forward?
options: Schedule a weekly manual audit of the Console for each project; require engineers to file a ticket when they make a manual change so the platform team can backfill it into Terraform
Run terraform plan in CI on a schedule and on PRs; investigate any non-empty plan, either import to keep the change or apply to remove it, and restrict Console write access via IAM
Delete and recreate the project from scratch using the Terraform module so any out-of-band changes are dropped, accepting the brief outage as the cost of consistency
Disable the Compute Engine firewall API at the org level and require all firewall changes to flow through a custom Cloud Function gated by Cloud Build approvals
explanation: Periodic terraform plan in CI is the standard drift-detection mechanism; once detected you decide to import (keep) or apply (remove), and IAM restrictions prevent recurrence. Manual audits don't scale and rely on humans self-reporting. Recreating projects is destructive and loses unrelated state. Disabling the firewall API breaks every legitimate workflow and is not how Org Policy or Console access should be constrained.

text: You want to prevent secrets (API keys, private keys) from ever reaching Artifact Registry by catching them at PR time. Which control is most effective?
options: Run an Artifact Analysis vulnerability scan on every pushed image and delete it from Artifact Registry if a credential pattern is found in the layer manifest
Add a secret-scanner step (gitleaks/trufflehog) to the Cloud Build PR pipeline that runs against the diff and fails the build on detection before the image is built
Rely on a documented PR review checklist that asks reviewers to grep for AWS_, GOOGLE_APPLICATION_CREDENTIALS, and BEGIN PRIVATE KEY in the changed files
Encrypt the entire source repository at rest with a CMEK so any leaked credentials are protected by Cloud KMS access controls in production
explanation: Catching the secret in the PR pipeline stops it before it ever lands in Git history or an image. Post-push image scanning is reactive — the secret is already in Git and possibly published. Reviewer checklists are unreliable for high-volume repos. CMEK on the repo encrypts data at rest but does nothing to prevent committing plaintext secrets that any read-permitted user can see.

text: Which Google Cloud feature continuously syncs Kubernetes manifests from a Git repository to one or more GKE clusters and reconciles drift back to the desired state?
options: Cloud Deploy
Config Sync (part of Anthos Config Management)
Cloud Build triggers
Artifact Registry
explanation: Config Sync is the GitOps controller for GKE/fleets. Cloud Deploy orchestrates progressive delivery (different concern). Build triggers run pipelines; Artifact Registry stores artifacts.

text: Which deployment strategy gradually shifts a small percentage of traffic to a new revision, monitors health, then progresses to full traffic — minimizing blast radius?
options: Recreate
Canary
Big-bang
AWS CodeDeploy LinearAllAtOnce
explanation: Canary releases progressively shift traffic with health checks at each step. Recreate causes downtime; big-bang has maximum blast radius; the AWS option is not a GCP service.

text: Which Google Cloud service automatically starts a CI pipeline when commits land on a specific branch of a connected source repository?
options: Cloud Scheduler
Cloud Build triggers
Cloud Run jobs
Eventarc only
explanation: Cloud Build triggers connect to GitHub/GitLab/Bitbucket/Cloud Source and start a build on configured branch/tag patterns. Cloud Scheduler runs cron, not VCS events; Cloud Run jobs are batch tasks; Eventarc routes events but doesn't define the build itself.

text: A regulated team must guarantee that production GKE only runs images that are: (1) built by their Cloud Build pipeline; (2) have SLSA-compliant provenance; (3) have NO critical CVEs at deploy time; (4) progress through dev → staging → prod with manual approval and full audit trail. Which combination meets ALL four?
options: Cloud Build with provenance; Artifact Registry with vulnerability scanning; Binary Authorization policy requiring the build attestor AND a vulnerability check attestor; Cloud Deploy pipeline with promotion approvals (audited via Cloud Audit Logs)
Manual `kubectl apply` after a code review
Cloud Build only; rely on engineers to check CVEs before deploy
Artifact Registry vulnerability scanning only
explanation: Binary Authorization composes multiple required attestors (build provenance + vulnerability gate); Cloud Deploy provides structured promotion with approvals; Audit Logs record every promotion. Manual apply lacks gating and audit; CB-only or scan-only each miss enforcement at admission.

text: A team has burned ~40% of their monthly 99.9% availability error budget in the first week due to two incidents. The next sprint is packed with risky feature work. Per SRE practice, what is the appropriate response?
options: Ignore the budget and ship the features anyway
Freeze risky feature work for the remainder of the period and prioritize reliability fixes; resume features when the budget recovers
Loosen the SLO to 99.5% so the budget appears healthier
Add more dashboards but no behavioral change
explanation: The whole point of an error budget is to create an explicit incentive to slow feature work when reliability suffers. Loosening the SLO is gaming the metric; ignoring or only adding dashboards changes nothing.

text: Which Google Cloud service runs jobs on a recurring cron schedule and can target HTTP endpoints, Pub/Sub, and App Engine?
options: Cloud Tasks
Cloud Scheduler
Cloud Workflows
Eventarc
explanation: Cloud Scheduler is the managed cron service. Cloud Tasks is for asynchronous task execution; Workflows orchestrates multi-step flows; Eventarc routes event-driven invocations.

text: A new Cloud Run revision is deployed and 5xx errors spike. Which fastest-to-root-cause approach combines the right Google Cloud signals?
options: Open a debugger and step through the application

In Cloud Monitoring view request count and error rate grouped by Cloud Run revision; in Error Reporting look for new exception groups; in Cloud Logging filter by `resource.type=cloud_run_revision` and `severity>=ERROR` for the suspect revision
Wait for the next status page update
Run a load test against the old revision
explanation: Grouping by revision in Monitoring isolates the offending version; Error Reporting clusters new exception types; revision-scoped log filtering finds the actual stack traces. The other options don't accelerate diagnosis.

text: Which postmortem action item is MOST likely to prevent recurrence of a class of incidents (vs. only the specific incident)?
options: Add a runbook entry describing the manual mitigation
Add an automated guardrail in CI/CD or platform code that makes the failure mode impossible (or detected pre-prod)
Send a company-wide email about the incident
Increase on-call rotation size
explanation: Systemic, automation-based action items (CI checks, admission policies, platform invariants) prevent the entire class of failure. Runbooks help react, emails inform, larger rotations spread load — none address the root systemic gap.

text: You need a Cloud Monitoring alerting policy that pages PagerDuty when CPU exceeds 80% for 5 minutes on a Compute Engine MIG. Which two pieces are required?
options: A metric-threshold condition (compute.googleapis.com/instance/cpu/utilization > 0.8 for 5m) AND a notification channel of type PagerDuty
A log-based metric only
A Cloud Function only
An Eventarc trigger only
explanation: Alerting policies are condition + notification channel. PagerDuty is a first-class notification channel type. The other items don't deliver alerting on their own.

text: An organization with 200 GCP projects across many tenants needs: (1) all Admin Activity + Data Access audit logs centralized for 7 years; (2) per-tenant application logs routed to per-tenant BigQuery datasets retained 90 days; (3) drop verbose DEBUG logs to control cost. Which design BEST meets all three?
options: An organization-level aggregated sink to a Cloud Logging bucket (7y retention) for audit logs; project-level sinks filtered by `labels.tenant_id` exporting to per-tenant BigQuery datasets (90-day partition expiry); exclusion filters on the `_Default` sink to drop DEBUG before ingest
One sink per project to a single BigQuery dataset, no exclusions
Export everything to GCS and grep with Dataproc
Disable Logging entirely and rely on application stdout
explanation: Org-level aggregated sinks centralize audit data; project-level sinks with tenant filters give per-tenant BigQuery routing; partition expiry handles 90-day retention; exclusions on `_Default` drop DEBUG before billable ingest. The other designs miss tenant routing, cost control, or governance.

text: Which Google Cloud tool would you use to find Cloud Run request logs with HTTP status 500 from the `payment` service in the last hour?
options: Cloud Logging — Log Explorer with filter `resource.type="cloud_run_revision" AND resource.labels.service_name="payment" AND httpRequest.status=500`
Cloud Trace
Cloud Profiler
BigQuery INFORMATION_SCHEMA
explanation: Log Explorer with the standard Cloud Run resource/labels filter is the direct path. Trace shows latency spans, Profiler shows CPU/memory profiles, and INFORMATION_SCHEMA queries BigQuery metadata.

text: A platform team needs unified observability across Go, Java, and Python services on GKE. Constraints: (1) only OpenTelemetry SDKs in apps (no vendor SDKs); (2) traces, metrics, AND logs must correlate by trace ID; (3) backend must be fully managed by Google; (4) no custom exporters in app code. Which design satisfies ALL four?
options: OpenTelemetry SDKs in apps export OTLP to a self-managed Jaeger + Prometheus + ELK stack

OpenTelemetry SDKs in apps send OTLP to an OpenTelemetry Collector (DaemonSet) which exports traces to Cloud Trace, metrics to Managed Service for Prometheus, and logs to Cloud Logging with trace_id field injection for correlation
Datadog Agent on every node forwarding to Datadog SaaS
Cloud Profiler as the single backend for traces, metrics, and logs
explanation: Apps stay pure-OTel via OTLP to a Collector that fans out to Google-managed backends; injecting trace_id into log entries enables cross-signal correlation. Self-managed stacks fail the 'fully managed' constraint; Datadog violates the OTel-SDK-only and managed-Google constraints; Profiler doesn't replace tracing/logging/metrics.

text: On a GKE Standard cluster, pods request 1 CPU each but actual utilization averages 30%. Nodes report 80% CPU allocatable used but only 24% real CPU. Which change reduces cost while preserving reliability?
options: Manually edit each Deployment to drop CPU requests by 60%, then rely on the HorizontalPodAutoscaler to add replicas if real load increases
Run Vertical Pod Autoscaler in 'Off' (recommendation) mode to learn right-sized requests, then apply them (or use 'Auto' mode where it's safe to evict pods)
Switch the workloads from CPU requests to BestEffort QoS by removing requests entirely; the scheduler will pack pods more tightly
Enable cluster-autoscaler in Optimize-Utilization profile and reduce min nodes; the autoscaler will consolidate underused pods automatically
explanation: VPA recommendations close the gap between requested and used CPU, letting more pods fit per node so the cluster shrinks — reliable because the recommendations are based on observed usage. Manually slashing requests guesses the new value and can starve burst traffic. Removing requests (BestEffort) breaks scheduling guarantees and HPA. Cluster-autoscaler reacts to *node* underuse but cannot consolidate pods that themselves over-request — the requests are the bottleneck.

text: A GKE Standard cluster has these workload patterns: 40% steady 24/7, 30% weekday business-hours, 30% unpredictable bursts. Which discount strategy minimizes cost without stranding commitments?
options: Buy resource-based 1-year CUDs sized to 100% of peak capacity in each region the cluster runs in
Size 1-year CUDs to the 40% always-on baseline; let business-hours load run on-demand (sustained-use discounts apply automatically); use Spot VMs for fault-tolerant burst workloads
Buy resource-based 3-year CUDs sized to the 70% baseline-plus-business-hours combined, accepting some over-commitment for the deeper discount
Use Spot VMs for the entire cluster; checkpoint workloads frequently and accept that the steady-state tier may restart on preemption
explanation: Sizing CUDs to the always-on baseline maximizes commitment value without stranding it; SUDs apply automatically to weekday on-demand usage; Spot is ideal for fault-tolerant bursts. Committing to peak (option A) strands ~60% of the commitment outside business hours. Three-year CUDs at 70% (option C) lock in capacity that the unpredictable burst tier won't consistently use. All-Spot (option D) breaks the steady 24/7 reliability requirement.

text: Your monthly GCP bill jumped 40%. You need to identify which projects, services, and SKUs drove the increase, with breakdowns by labels (cost-allocation tags). Which tool is the right starting point?
options: Cloud Asset Inventory
Cloud Billing reports + BigQuery billing export, sliced by project, service, SKU, and labels
Security Command Center
Cloud Trace
explanation: Cloud Billing reports give built-in slicing by project/service/SKU/label; the BigQuery billing export lets you query the full detailed cost dataset for ad-hoc breakdowns. Asset Inventory tracks resource state, not cost; SCC is security; Trace is latency.

text: An enterprise has 1,200 VMs on-prem (mixed Linux/Windows), 40 Oracle DBs, ~6 PB of unstructured files, and a SAP S/4HANA estate. They need a 24-month phased migration with: Migration Center for assessment + wave planning, an execution tool for VMs, a managed path for Oracle (no app changes), and a continuous on-prem→GCS path for the file estate during the long tail. Which combination is BEST?
options: Migration Center → Migrate to Virtual Machines (VMs) → Bare Metal Solution (Oracle, no app changes) → Storage Transfer Service (on-prem agent) for files; SAP migrated via Google Cloud's certified SAP migration program
Migration Center → Cloud Run for everything → Cloud SQL for Oracle → gsutil one-shot copy for files
Database Migration Service for VMs and Oracle → Storage Transfer Service for files → SAP rebuilt as microservices
Skip Migration Center; lift-and-shift with `gcloud compute import` and migrate Oracle to Spanner
explanation: Bare Metal Solution is the canonical zero-app-change path for Oracle on GCP; Migrate to VMs handles the VM fleet; STS with the on-prem agent is the supported continuous file-copy path; SAP has a dedicated Google Cloud certified migration program. DMS is for relational databases only — it cannot migrate VMs. Cloud Run/Spanner aren't drop-in replacements for VMs/Oracle.

text: A company runs prod, staging, and dev across 4 business units. Requirements: (1) prod must be isolated from non-prod in the IAM blast radius; (2) BU finance leads must own their BU's billing and IAM but not other BUs'; (3) security team needs read-only across everything; (4) common shared services (logging, networking) must be reusable across all BUs. Which folder layout BEST fits?
options: Org → one folder per project; flat

Org → `bootstrap/`, `common/` (logging, networking host projects), `bu-{a,b,c,d}/` each containing `prod/`, `nonprod/` subfolders with projects underneath; org-level viewer for security; per-BU billing sub-accounts; org policies on `prod/` folders
Org → one folder per environment (prod/dev/staging) with all BU projects mixed inside
Separate Google Cloud Organizations per BU
explanation: BU folders provide IAM isolation and per-BU ownership; the `prod/` subfolder is the natural attach point for stricter org policies and IAM. A `common/` folder hosts Shared VPC and log sinks reusable by every BU. Org-level read role gives security the cross-cutting view. Flat or env-only layouts can't satisfy (1) and (2) simultaneously; separate Orgs fragment governance and shared services.

text: An on-prem data center connects to a GCP VPC via two 10 Gbps Dedicated Interconnects in different metro facilities. Requirements: (1) 99.99% SLA for production traffic; (2) failover MUST be automatic with sub-minute convergence; (3) routing must prefer the lower-latency link; (4) plan must survive a full metro outage. Which design is BEST?
options: Both Interconnects in the same metro for simpler ops; static routes; manual failover
Two Interconnects in separate metros, each on its own Cloud Router; BGP with MED to prefer the lower-latency path; redundant edge devices on the customer side; HA VPN as warm backup
A single 100 Gbps Dedicated Interconnect with no backup
Cloud NAT plus a public IP, relying on the public internet
explanation: The 99.99% Dedicated Interconnect topology requires links in two separate metros, each on its own Cloud Router, with BGP for sub-minute convergence and MED to express path preference. HA VPN as warm backup is a recommended belt-and-suspenders pattern. Same-metro defeats the SLA; single link has no redundancy; Cloud NAT is for outbound public traffic.

text: Which Google Cloud load balancer terminates TLS at the edge, supports global anycast IP, integrates Cloud Armor, and routes HTTP(S) traffic to backends in multiple regions?
options: Internal TCP/UDP Load Balancer
Global external Application Load Balancer
AWS Application Load Balancer
Regional Network Passthrough Load Balancer
explanation: Global external ALB is the only GCP option that combines anycast IP, edge TLS termination, multi-region routing, and Cloud Armor integration. Internal LBs don't serve external traffic; the AWS option is not GCP; regional passthrough NLBs are L4 single-region.

text: A latency-sensitive trading service must run with: (1) p99 < 5 ms intra-rack latency; (2) consistent CPU performance (no noisy neighbors); (3) hardware-level isolation for regulatory reasons; (4) ability to bring custom licensed software bound to specific physical CPUs. Which compute option is BEST?
options: Cloud Run with min-instances and 2nd-gen execution
GKE Autopilot with guaranteed QoS pods
Compute Engine sole-tenant nodes with compact placement policy and per-VM licensing pinned to the physical host
Spot VMs in a regional MIG
explanation: Sole-tenant nodes give physical-host isolation, compact placement keeps VMs in the same rack for sub-5ms latency, and per-VM licensing is supported. Cloud Run can't guarantee hardware isolation or compact placement. Autopilot abstracts nodes away. Spot VMs are preemptible — wrong workload class.

text: An application stores millions of small thumbnails. Reads are heavy in the first 30 days, then drop to <1/month, but objects must remain instantly available for 5 years. Which configuration minimizes lifetime cost?
options: Standard storage forever
Standard for 30 days; lifecycle rule transitioning to Nearline at 30 days, Coldline at 90, Archive at 365
Archive storage from day 1
Move objects to Bigtable after 30 days
explanation: Object Lifecycle Management age-based class transitions match the access-frequency curve. Archive from day 1 incurs heavy retrieval cost during the hot first 30 days. Bigtable is a NoSQL DB, not blob storage. Standard forever is the most expensive long-tail option.

text: Which Google Cloud IAM principle should be the default when granting access to a service account?
options: Grant Owner role for simplicity
Grant only the predefined or custom role(s) the workload needs (least privilege)
Grant `roles/editor` to every workload
Grant the AWS Administrator policy
explanation: Least privilege is the IAM foundation: bind only the roles required for the workload. Owner/Editor grant far more than needed; the AWS option is not a GCP concept.

text: A US healthcare org processing PHI on GCP must meet: (1) signed BAA; (2) no data egress to non-HIPAA-eligible services; (3) all admin actions audited including reads; (4) network-isolated PHI services; (5) quarterly attestation of effective controls with low manual effort. Which combination BEST satisfies all five?
options: Sign a BAA, restrict to HIPAA-eligible services via org policy `gcp.restrictServiceUsage`, enable Data Access audit logs, deploy a VPC Service Controls perimeter around PHI projects, use Security Command Center Premium for continuous compliance monitoring
Sign a BAA and rely on default Google encryption — that's all HIPAA requires
Skip the BAA but enable CMEK and VPC-SC
Deploy everything in a single project with public buckets but long random object names
explanation: All five controls map directly: BAA (legal), `restrictServiceUsage` enforces eligible-only services, Data Access logs cover read-side auditing, VPC-SC isolates PHI from data exfiltration, and SCC Premium provides continuous-compliance evidence for attestation. The other options each violate at least one HIPAA-on-GCP requirement.

text: A Cloud Run service stores a PostgreSQL connection string in Secret Manager. The team wants: (1) version pinning (so a bad rotation can be rolled back instantly); (2) the secret never written to disk in the container; (3) automatic propagation to a new revision when the secret version changes; (4) IAM-scoped per environment. Which configuration BEST meets all four?
options: Mount the secret as a versioned environment variable referencing `projects/.../secrets/db/versions/3` (pinned), grant `roles/secretmanager.secretAccessor` to the per-env Cloud Run service account, and redeploy via a Cloud Deploy pipeline triggered by a new secret version
Bake the password into the container image
Store it in a public GCS bucket
Use the same `:latest` alias for all environments and skip IAM scoping
explanation: Pinning a specific version enables instant rollback (just redeploy with the prior version); env-var injection avoids on-disk writes; per-env service accounts give IAM scoping; pipeline-triggered redeploys propagate version bumps. `:latest` defeats pinning; baked-in or public storage are obvious anti-patterns.

text: A streaming pipeline ingests 250k events/sec from Pub/Sub. Requirements: (1) exactly-once writes to BigQuery; (2) 5-minute tumbling windows joined with a slowly-changing dimension table in Cloud Spanner; (3) handle late events up to 30 minutes via watermarks; (4) backfills must reuse the same code as streaming. Which design is BEST?
options: Cloud Functions per event writing to BigQuery streaming inserts; cron job for late events

Dataflow streaming (Apache Beam) with the BigQuery Storage Write API in EXACTLY_ONCE mode; side-input from Spanner via JDBC IO; allowed lateness = 30m; the same Beam pipeline executed in batch mode for backfills
Dataproc Spark Streaming with at-least-once writes; manual dedup later
Cloud Run with manual sharding by message attribute
explanation: Beam unifies streaming and batch (same code for backfill); BigQuery Storage Write API supports exactly-once; allowed lateness handles the 30-min watermark; Spanner side-input provides the dimension join. Cloud Functions/Cloud Run lack windowing/watermarks; at-least-once Spark requires post-hoc dedup.

text: Which Google Cloud service provides serverless, petabyte-scale SQL analytics with a separation of compute (slots) and storage?
options: Bigtable
Cloud SQL
BigQuery
Amazon Redshift
explanation: BigQuery is the serverless analytics engine with separate compute (slots) and columnar storage. Bigtable is a wide-column NoSQL store; Cloud SQL is a transactional RDBMS; Redshift is AWS.

text: A platform team is choosing between Apigee X, API Gateway, and Cloud Endpoints (ESPv2). Requirements: (1) monetization with rate plans; (2) developer portal with self-service onboarding; (3) advanced policies (mediation, transformation, OAuth2 + JWT); (4) hybrid deployment to also front on-prem APIs; (5) lowest ops effort. Which is BEST?
options: API Gateway — the GCP-managed gateway
Cloud Endpoints (ESPv2) running in GKE
Apigee X (managed, multi-tenant)
Build a custom NGINX cluster
explanation: Only Apigee X provides monetization, a developer portal, advanced policy chains, and Apigee Hybrid for fronting on-prem APIs — all fully managed. API Gateway and Endpoints are simpler and cheaper but lack monetization, portal, and hybrid; NGINX is fully self-managed.

text: Which Google Cloud product is the simplest fit for an event-driven function that runs in response to a new file landing in Cloud Storage?
options: Compute Engine VM with a polling cron
Cloud Run function (Cloud Functions) triggered by the GCS finalize event
GKE DaemonSet polling the bucket
AWS Lambda with an S3 trigger
explanation: Cloud Functions / Cloud Run functions natively support GCS finalize event triggers via Eventarc — no polling, no cluster, no servers. The other options either poll or are non-GCP.

text: A team is designing observability for a new Cloud Run service. They want: (1) request-based SLOs in Cloud Monitoring; (2) structured JSON logs correlated to traces by trace ID; (3) one dashboard combining latency, error rate, saturation; (4) alerts wired to PagerDuty AND a chat channel; (5) a runbook URL surfaced on every page. Which approach BEST satisfies all five?
options: Plaintext logs, Stackdriver default dashboards, single email channel

Define a Cloud Monitoring SLO + multi-burn-rate policy; configure structured JSON logging with `logging.googleapis.com/trace` field; build a custom dashboard with latency/error/saturation widgets; create PagerDuty AND chat notification channels on the alerting policy; add `documentation.content` with the runbook URL
Skip SLOs; rely only on uptime checks
Use Cloud Profiler as the alerting backend
explanation: Each control maps to a specific Cloud Monitoring/Logging feature: SLO API, trace-field correlation, dashboard widgets, multiple notification channels per policy, and `documentation.content` for runbook links. The other options each miss multiple requirements.

text: Per Architecture Framework operational excellence, which practice should be in place BEFORE a service handles production traffic?
options: Defined SLIs/SLOs, runbooks for the top failure modes, and a tested rollback path
Only a status-page page
Only a postmortem template
Manual deploys reviewed by an architect each time
explanation: The framework's pre-prod readiness checklist centers on measurable SLIs/SLOs, runbooks, and rollback. Templates and manual reviews don't substitute for instrumentation and reversibility.

text: Which Google Cloud resource lets a network team own subnets and firewall rules in one project while application teams deploy workloads in their own projects against those subnets?
options: VPC Network Peering
Shared VPC (host project + service projects)
Cloud NAT
AWS Transit Gateway
explanation: Shared VPC is purpose-built for this split-ownership model. Peering connects equals; Cloud NAT is for outbound public traffic; Transit Gateway is AWS.

text: A team's CI builds for many GCP projects. Requirements: (1) no long-lived service-account keys anywhere; (2) per-environment principle of least privilege (dev pipeline can't deploy to prod); (3) auditable mapping from a specific GitHub Actions workflow run to the GCP credential it used; (4) cross-org reuse (multiple GitHub orgs). Which design BEST meets all four?
options: One JSON SA key per environment, stored as a GitHub org secret

A single Workload Identity Federation pool with attribute conditions binding `repository`/`workflow`/`environment` claims to per-env service accounts; each env's SA has narrow IAM; provider config logs the OIDC subject for audit
Personal access tokens for each developer used by the workflow
Disable WIF and use `gcloud auth login` interactively in CI
explanation: WIF attribute conditions on `repository`, `workflow`, and `environment` allow fine-grained per-env mappings without keys; OIDC subjects appear in Cloud Audit Logs for traceability; pools can accept multiple GitHub orgs as providers. Stored SA keys and PATs both violate the 'no long-lived key' rule.

text: A platform team manages 30 GKE clusters across regions and tenants. Requirements: (1) one source of truth for cluster + namespace configs (GitOps); (2) prevent non-compliant resources at admission (no privileged pods, required labels); (3) per-team namespaces with quotas; (4) centralized policy reporting across the fleet. Which combination is BEST?
options: kubectl apply from each engineer's laptop; trust but verify

Add the clusters to a GKE fleet; enable Config Sync (root + namespace repos), Policy Controller (constraint templates for pod security + label policies), tenant-namespace ResourceQuotas, and the fleet-wide Policy Controller dashboard for posture
Helm-only deployments with no admission controls
ArgoCD per cluster with no cross-cluster reporting
explanation: Config Sync = GitOps source of truth; Policy Controller = OPA Gatekeeper for admission constraints; ResourceQuotas isolate tenants; the fleet Policy Controller dashboard gives unified posture. The other options each miss admission control or central reporting.

text: A team rolls out a new GKE service with these requirements: (1) progressive delivery 5% → 25% → 100% with a 30-minute soak at each step; (2) automatic abort if SLO error budget burn rate exceeds 5×; (3) reproducible deploy artifacts with attestations; (4) one approval gate before prod. Which Google Cloud design BEST fits?
options: kubectl apply with manual percentage edits; pager on errors

Cloud Deploy pipeline with a canary strategy (5/25/100), `verify` jobs that query Cloud Monitoring SLO burn rate and fail (auto-abort); Binary Authorization attestor on the image; manual `promote` approval before the prod target
Blue/green with no health gating
Recreate strategy in production
explanation: Cloud Deploy canary supports phased percentages; `verify` jobs can call Monitoring and fail to abort the rollout; Binary Authorization gives the attestation requirement; promotion approvals satisfy the manual gate. kubectl/recreate/blue-green-without-gating each miss multiple requirements.

text: Which deployment pattern is characterized by maintaining two full production environments and switching all traffic from one to the other in a single cutover, with the prior environment kept warm for instant rollback?
options: Canary
Blue/green
Rolling update
Recreate
explanation: Blue/green keeps two parallel stacks and cuts over via a router/LB change. Canary shifts a small percentage gradually; rolling does instance-by-instance; recreate causes downtime.

text: A backwards-incompatible API v2 must be released alongside v1 for 60 days.
