Constraints: (1) shared underlying database; (2) zero downtime; (3) per-tenant feature-flag opt-in to v2; (4) ability to dark-launch v2 read paths to all tenants for shadow comparison before any tenant opts in; (5) instant kill-switch on v2. Which strategy BEST fits?
options: Big-bang cutover during a maintenance window

Run v1 and v2 deployments behind a global ALB; use a feature-flag service (e.g. config-driven) for tenant opt-in; mirror a copy of read traffic to v2 (shadow) via traffic-mirroring or app-level fanout; use the expand–contract DB migration pattern (additive schema first, dual-write, then contract); flag-based instant kill-switch
Replace v1 with v2 atomically and rely on rollbacks
Deploy v2 only to a different region and use DNS round-robin
explanation: Expand–contract makes the shared DB safe; ALB + feature flags handle tenant opt-in and kill-switch; shadow traffic enables comparison without user impact. The other options either cause downtime, lack the kill-switch, or break sticky sessions.

text: A regulated team needs CI that: (1) builds in an isolated, ephemeral worker (no shared mutable state); (2) signs every artifact with SLSA L3-grade provenance; (3) blocks builds that pull from non-allowlisted base images; (4) integrates with Artifact Registry remote repos to proxy upstream packages; (5) zero long-lived credentials. Which Google Cloud setup BEST satisfies all five?
options: Self-hosted Jenkins on a long-running VM with shared workspaces

Cloud Build with private pools (ephemeral workers), SLSA build provenance enabled, an org policy + custom build step that fails on disallowed base images, Artifact Registry remote/virtual repos as the only proxy, and Workload Identity Federation for any external auth
GitHub-hosted runners with stored SA JSON keys
Bash scripts run on developers' laptops
explanation: Cloud Build private pools give ephemeral isolated workers; SLSA provenance is built-in; Artifact Registry remote repos proxy upstreams under your control; an allowlist check fails non-compliant builds; WIF removes long-lived keys. The other options violate at least one of the five constraints.

text: An ML platform must serve burst training jobs that need 8 H100 GPUs each, on-demand within 5 minutes, in a region where on-demand H100 capacity is scarce. Constraints: (1) jobs are not fault-tolerant (no Spot); (2) cost must beat 1-year CUD purchase; (3) capacity must be guaranteed for the next 8 weeks. Which approach BEST fits?
options: Buy 1-year CUDs for 8 H100s
Use Dynamic Workload Scheduler in `flex-start` mode for queued capacity, plus a future reservation for the 8-week window when capacity must be guaranteed; fall back to on-demand otherwise
Rely on on-demand only and pray
Rewrite jobs for Spot VMs
explanation: DWS flex-start queues for capacity efficiently for non-urgent jobs; future reservations explicitly guarantee capacity for a known window without committing for a year. CUD is too long; on-demand isn't reliable in scarce regions; Spot violates the no-fault-tolerance constraint.

text: A Sev-1 incident is in progress: 5xx errors are spiking after a deploy, but the on-call notices the new revision also depends on a freshly migrated database schema. Three hypotheses are open. Per SRE practice, which sequence is BEST?
options: Convene a war room and start root-cause analysis before any user-facing action

Immediately mitigate by rolling back to the prior known-good revision (and revert the DB migration if reversible) to stop user pain; communicate status; THEN diagnose using logs/traces/metrics filtered to the suspect revision; write a blameless postmortem
Page more engineers and wait
Scale up replicas and hope the errors clear
explanation: SRE: mitigate → communicate → diagnose → postmortem. Rollback (and reversible DB revert) is the standard mitigation. Convening RCA first prolongs user pain; scaling without diagnosis is cargo-cult.

text: After three Sev-1s in 30 days from the same root cause class (a missing pre-deploy schema check), which set of action items BEST follows SRE postmortem guidance?
options: Email a reminder to all engineers to be careful

Add an automated pre-deploy schema-compatibility check in the CI/CD pipeline that fails the build on incompatibility; add a Cloud Deploy `verify` job that runs the check; track an SLO on schema-related incidents; add a blameless postmortem retrospective scheduled for 60 days
Forbid all schema changes
Add a runbook step that the on-call must remember during deploys
explanation: Systemic action items (pipeline check, verify job, tracked SLO, follow-up) prevent the entire class. Reminders and runbook-only steps depend on humans remembering at the wrong time; banning schema changes is impractical.

text: A platform team wants metrics that: (1) use PromQL; (2) are queryable globally across many projects; (3) retain raw resolution for 6 weeks and downsampled for 24 months; (4) accept Prometheus remote_write from on-prem clusters too. Which design BEST fits?
options: Run a self-managed Prometheus + Thanos cluster on GKE

Google Cloud Managed Service for Prometheus with a metrics scope spanning all projects; collectors in each cluster (in-cluster managed collection) and remote_write from on-prem; rely on GMP's long retention
Cloud Logging only — metrics aren't needed
Send all metrics to BigQuery via Pub/Sub and query with SQL
explanation: GMP is PromQL-compatible, supports remote_write from on-prem, has long native retention, and metrics scopes provide cross-project query. Self-managed Thanos is expensive ops; Logging isn't a metrics store; raw BigQuery loses PromQL.

text: A platform team wants a single dashboard that combines: (1) a PromQL p99 latency widget from GMP; (2) a Cloud Logging-based-metric error count; (3) an SLO error budget burn-down; (4) a custom log query as a table. Constraints: must be defined as code (reviewable in Git) and deployed via CI. Which approach BEST fits?
options: Click around the Cloud Console and screenshot it for review

Define the dashboard as a Cloud Monitoring Dashboard JSON (or via Terraform `google_monitoring_dashboard`) combining PromQL widgets, log-based-metric widgets, SLO widgets, and a logs-panel widget; commit to Git; deploy via CI
Export to Looker Studio and email PDFs
Use Grafana with no version control
explanation: Cloud Monitoring dashboards support PromQL widgets, log-based-metric widgets, SLO burn widgets, and logs panels — all expressible in JSON / Terraform for GitOps. The other options violate the as-code requirement.

text: Which Google Cloud product collects CPU and heap profiles continuously from production with negligible overhead?
options: Cloud Trace
Cloud Profiler
Cloud Monitoring uptime checks
BigQuery
explanation: Cloud Profiler is the always-on, low-overhead production profiler. Trace is for distributed request spans; uptime checks probe endpoints; BigQuery is analytics.

text: A team wants to surface the most recent commit SHA inside a Cloud Run revision so an operator can confirm what's deployed. Which is the simplest GCP-native pattern?
options: Hard-code the SHA in source
Pass the commit SHA as a Cloud Run env var at deploy time (e.g., `--set-env-vars=GIT_SHA=$COMMIT_SHA` from Cloud Build) and expose it on a `/version` endpoint
Manually update a wiki after each deploy
Store the SHA in Firestore at runtime
explanation: Env vars set at deploy time travel with the revision and can be exposed by `/version`. Hard-coding requires rebuilds; wikis drift; Firestore is over-engineering.

text: A GKE Standard cluster must support spiky workloads cost-effectively while meeting these constraints: (1) cold-start under 60 seconds for new pods; (2) no permanently overprovisioned headroom; (3) per-pod memory right-sizing without restarting pods; (4) automatic node-pool optimization across machine types. Which combination BEST fits?
options: Static node pools with manual sizing
Cluster Autoscaler + Horizontal Pod Autoscaler + Vertical Pod Autoscaler in `Initial`/`Auto` mode + Node Auto-Provisioning to pick machine shapes; image streaming for fast pod start
Spot VMs only with no autoscaler
GKE Autopilot, but disable HPA and VPA
explanation: CA + HPA + VPA + NAP cover horizontal, vertical, and node-shape elasticity; image streaming addresses cold-start. Static sizing wastes money; Spot-only risks the cold-start SLO; disabling HPA/VPA in Autopilot defeats the purpose.

text: An org wants to maximize discounts across a large GCE fleet but avoid stranding commitments as workloads shift between projects. Constraints: (1) shareable across projects under a billing account; (2) flexibility across machine families; (3) lowest discount-management overhead. Which choice BEST fits?
options: Resource-based committed use discounts purchased per-project per-machine-type
Flexible (spend-based) committed use discounts at the billing-account level, automatically applied across projects and eligible families
Spot VMs only
Sustained-use discounts only
explanation: Flexible CUDs commit to a dollar amount of compute spend and apply across projects under the billing account and across eligible families — least likely to be stranded as workloads move. Resource-based CUDs are project- and shape-locked; Spot/SUD don't satisfy the commitment-discount goal at scale.

text: A platform team is building a FinOps capability. Requirements: (1) per-team chargeback by GCP labels; (2) anomaly detection on daily spend; (3) forecast budget alerts at 50/80/100% with Pub/Sub for automation; (4) ad-hoc SQL slicing of detailed cost line items; (5) recommendations for rightsizing. Which combination BEST fits?
options: Cloud Billing reports only

Cloud Billing budgets (with Pub/Sub on threshold) + BigQuery detailed billing export (for SQL + label-based chargeback) + Cloud Monitoring anomaly detection on daily-spend log-based metric + Active Assist Recommender (cost recommendations)
Manual spreadsheet maintained weekly
Cloud Asset Inventory exports only
explanation: Each requirement maps to a feature: budgets+Pub/Sub for alerts/automation, BigQuery export for chargeback/SQL, Monitoring anomaly detection on a derived metric, and Recommender for rightsizing. Reports/spreadsheets/CAI alone miss most requirements.

text: Which statement BEST describes a foundation model in the context of generative AI?
options: A small task-specific model trained from scratch for a single business problem
A large model pre-trained on broad data at scale, designed to be adapted (e.g., via prompting or fine-tuning) to many downstream tasks
A rules-based expert system
A traditional regression model used for forecasting numeric values
explanation: Foundation models are large, broadly-trained models intended for adaptation to many downstream tasks. The other options describe narrow, task-specific or non-generative approaches.

doc_links.title: Generative AI on Vertex AI overview

text: A retailer wants to recommend products to shoppers without using historical labeled purchase outcomes — instead, the goal is to discover groups of similar customers from raw behavior data. Which machine learning approach is the BEST fit?
options: Supervised learning with labeled purchase outcomes
Unsupervised learning (e.g., clustering) on the unlabeled behavior data
Reinforcement learning with a reward function based on revenue
Symbolic rule writing by domain experts
explanation: Discovering groupings in unlabeled data is the canonical job of unsupervised learning (clustering). Supervised needs labels; reinforcement requires a defined reward loop and environment; rule writing isn't ML.

doc_links.title: Types of ML (Google ML crash course)

text: A regulated insurer must select a foundation model for an internal claims-summarization product. Requirements: (1) handles ~200-page PDFs in a single pass; (2) supports a signed BAA-equivalent data-handling agreement and never trains on customer data; (3) deployable in a specific region for data residency; (4) low latency for interactive review; (5) cost predictable at scale. Which set of selection criteria BEST aligns with Google's guidance for choosing a foundation model?
options: Pick the model with the most Twitter buzz
Evaluate modality, context window, security/privacy posture, regional availability and reliability, performance/latency, cost, and customization (fine-tuning) needs against the use case requirements
Always pick the largest model regardless of cost or latency
Always pick the cheapest model regardless of context window
explanation: The exam guide explicitly lists modality, context window, security, availability/reliability, cost, performance, and fine-tuning/customization as the model-selection considerations — they map 1:1 to the five requirements. Buzz, size-only, or cost-only heuristics ignore the constraints.

text: Which is the BEST example of unstructured data?
options: Rows in a Cloud SQL transactions table
A scanned PDF contract and a customer support call recording
A BigQuery table of daily sales aggregates
A CSV of product SKUs and prices
explanation: PDFs and audio recordings have no fixed schema — classic unstructured data. SQL rows, BigQuery tables, and CSVs are structured.

text: A team wants to fine-tune a model with supervised examples but their internal documents have no answers attached. According to the exam guide's data taxonomy, what do they need before supervised fine-tuning is feasible?
options: More unlabeled data only
Labeled data — input/output pairs that demonstrate the desired behavior
A larger context window only
A different programming language
explanation: Supervised fine-tuning requires labeled examples (input + desired output). Unlabeled data alone supports unsupervised or self-supervised approaches, not supervised tuning.

text: Per the gen AI landscape used in the exam guide, which layer is BEST described as 'autonomous reasoning systems that use tools to take actions on behalf of a user'?
options: Infrastructure
Models
Agents
Applications
explanation: Agents are the autonomous, tool-using layer. Infrastructure is the underlying compute/network; Models are the foundation models themselves; Applications are end-user products built on top.

text: A team needs to generate short-form marketing videos from a text brief. Which Google foundation model family is the BEST fit?
options: Gemini
Gemma
Imagen
Veo
explanation: Veo is Google's video-generation foundation model. Gemini is multimodal but primarily text/code; Gemma is a family of open models; Imagen generates still images.

doc_links.title: Generative AI models on Vertex AI

text: An ML team wants to deploy a small, openly-licensed model on their own infrastructure (including on-prem GPUs) for a privacy-sensitive workload, while still benefiting from Google research. Which Google model family is the BEST fit?
options: Gemini 1.5 Pro via Vertex AI only
Gemma — Google's family of open models intended for self-hosted and on-prem deployment
Imagen, used for image classification
Veo for text generation
explanation: Gemma is Google's open-weights model family, designed to be downloaded and run anywhere — the right answer for self-hosted/on-prem. Gemini is offered as a managed API; Imagen is image generation; Veo is video.

text: Which Google Cloud capability BEST exemplifies the 'AI-optimized infrastructure' pillar that the exam guide highlights as a Google Cloud strength for gen AI?
options: Cloud Storage Standard buckets
AI Hypercomputer architecture combining custom-designed TPUs, GPUs, high-bandwidth networking, and optimized software
Cloud DNS
Cloud Build private pools
explanation: AI Hypercomputer is Google's purpose-built, integrated AI infrastructure combining TPUs/GPUs, networking, storage, and software — the canonical answer for 'AI-optimized infrastructure'. The other services exist on GCP but aren't the AI-infra pillar.

text: A non-technical employee wants to create a reusable, customized version of Gemini that always answers in their company's tone of voice and references a fixed set of instructions — without writing code. Which capability is the BEST fit?
options: Spin up a Vertex AI training job
Create a Gem in the Gemini app (Gemini Advanced)
Deploy a custom model with Cloud Run
Write a Python notebook in Colab
explanation: Gems are the no-code, reusable custom personas in the Gemini app, exactly designed for this. The other options require engineering effort and aren't the prebuilt offering.

text: A contact-center leader wants AI to (a) suggest responses to live agents in real time, (b) automate post-call analytics for QA and trends, and (c) deflect routine calls to a self-service voice/chat bot. Which Google Cloud product family is the BEST fit?
options: Vertex AI Workbench notebooks
Customer Engagement Suite — Conversational Agents, Agent Assist, Conversational Insights, and Contact Center as a Service
Cloud Translation API only
BigQuery ML
explanation: CES is the integrated portfolio that maps directly to all three needs (Agent Assist for live suggestions, Conversational Insights for analytics, Conversational Agents for self-service). The other options are general-purpose tools, not the contact-center suite.

text: A team wants to expose a chat experience grounded in their internal product documentation, with the lowest engineering effort and a managed retrieval index they don't have to operate. Which Google Cloud offering is the BEST fit?
options: Build a custom vector index on a self-managed PostgreSQL instance
Use prebuilt RAG with Vertex AI Search as the retrieval layer behind a Gemini-powered app
Train a model from scratch on the docs
Email the docs to users
explanation: Prebuilt RAG with Vertex AI Search provides a managed retrieval index plus generative answers — minimal engineering. Self-managed vector DBs and from-scratch training violate the lowest-effort and managed-retrieval requirements.

text: Which Vertex AI capability gives users a curated catalog of Google's first-party models alongside open and partner models that can be deployed from a single console?
options: Vertex AI Workbench
Model Garden
Cloud Storage
Looker Studio
explanation: Model Garden is the curated catalog of first-party, open, and partner models inside Vertex AI. Workbench is a notebook environment; the others are unrelated.

text: A team is choosing where to prototype prompts and agent flows. They need an enterprise-grade environment with IAM, VPC-SC, and integration with Vertex AI services. Which option is the BEST fit?
options: Google AI Studio (consumer-grade prototyping)
Vertex AI Studio (enterprise, integrated with Vertex AI, IAM, and VPC-SC)
A local Jupyter notebook with API keys in plaintext
A public Colab notebook
explanation: Vertex AI Studio is the enterprise-targeted prompt/model studio with IAM, VPC-SC, and Vertex integration. Google AI Studio is the consumer/free-tier counterpart for quick experiments without enterprise governance.

doc_links.title: Google AI Studio vs Vertex AI

text: Users complain that an LLM-powered assistant sometimes invents company policies that don't exist. Per Google's recommended practices, which combination is the BEST first response?
options: Tell the LLM to 'try harder' in the system prompt
Add grounding (RAG against the policy corpus) plus prompt engineering, and add a human-in-the-loop review for high-risk responses
Switch to a smaller model
Disable safety settings to allow more creative answers
explanation: Hallucinations are addressed by grounding the model on authoritative data (RAG), tightening prompts, and HITL for high-risk paths. Smaller models or weaker safety settings make the problem worse.

text: A prompt designer adds three example input/output pairs before the actual user query so the model imitates the desired output format. Which prompting technique is this?
options: Zero-shot prompting
Few-shot prompting
Chain-of-thought prompting
ReAct prompting
explanation: Few-shot = supplying a small number (here three) of example pairs in-context. Zero-shot has no examples; chain-of-thought asks the model to reason step-by-step; ReAct interleaves reasoning with tool actions.

text: A complex multi-step reasoning task (e.g., 'plan a 3-stop business trip, check live weather, and book hotels within budget') needs the model to BOTH reason about steps AND call external tools/APIs to fetch real data. Which prompting technique BEST fits?
options: Zero-shot prompting
Role prompting alone
ReAct prompting (interleave Reasoning steps with Action/tool calls and observations)
Few-shot prompting only
explanation: ReAct (Reason + Act) is specifically designed for tasks that mix step-by-step reasoning with tool invocation against the external environment. Zero-shot/role/few-shot don't model the action loop.

text: A user asks a Gemini-powered assistant questions about events that happened after the model's training cutoff. The team wants the model to answer using up-to-date public web information without building their own retrieval pipeline. Which Google Cloud grounding option is the BEST fit?
options: Pre-built RAG with Vertex AI Search
Custom RAG APIs against an internal corpus
Grounding with Google Search
Disable grounding entirely
explanation: 'Grounding with Google Search' is the managed offering for grounding model output on fresh public web data — no retrieval pipeline to build. The Vertex AI Search/RAG-API options are for first-party enterprise data.

text: Which gen AI sampling parameter most directly controls how 'creative' (more diverse) vs. 'focused' (more deterministic) a model's responses are?
options: Temperature
Maximum output tokens
Project ID
Region
explanation: Temperature controls randomness/diversity in token sampling. Max output tokens caps response length; project ID and region are infrastructure settings.

text: A leadership team is launching its first transformational gen AI initiative. Per Google's recommended steps, which sequence BEST reflects how to choose and integrate a gen AI solution?
options: Pick the trendiest model first, then look for a problem to solve

Define the business need and constraints → identify candidate gen AI solution types (create/summarize/discover/automate) → choose model + platform against those constraints → integrate with change management → measure impact via defined KPIs
Train a model from scratch before defining the use case
Skip measurement; gen AI value is intangible
explanation: The exam guide explicitly lists business need → solution-type fit → choose the right solution → integrate → measure as the recommended sequence. The other options invert the order or skip critical steps.

text: What is the purpose of Google's Secure AI Framework (SAIF)?
options: A pricing model for Vertex AI
A conceptual framework for securing AI systems across the ML lifecycle (e.g., data, model, deployment, and monitoring risks)
A specific Cloud Run product
A replacement for IAM
explanation: SAIF is Google's published framework for thinking about AI-system security across the lifecycle. It is not a product, a pricing model, or a replacement for IAM.

text: An enterprise launching a customer-facing Gemini-grounded assistant needs to defend against the SAIF-style risks of (1) prompt-injection-driven data exfiltration, (2) excessive permissions on the agent's service account, (3) unmonitored anomalous usage, and (4) tampering with the underlying training/grounding data. Which combination of Google Cloud controls BEST addresses all four?
options: Rely on the model provider to handle all security

Input/output filtering and safety settings + least-privilege IAM on the agent's service account + Security Command Center + Cloud Audit Logs + access controls on the grounding data store (e.g., Cloud Storage IAM and VPC Service Controls)
Disable logging to reduce blast radius
Make the agent's service account an Owner for simplicity
explanation: Each requirement maps to a control: filtering+safety for prompt injection, least-privilege IAM for permissions, SCC + audit logs for anomaly detection, and access controls + VPC-SC for data tampering. The other options either offload responsibility or violate basic security principles.

text: A bank is deploying a gen AI assistant that influences loan-eligibility conversations. Per Google's responsible AI guidance, which practice is MOST important to implement?
options: Maximize automation by removing all human review
Document data sources, evaluate for bias/fairness, provide explainability for outputs that affect customers, and define accountability owners — keeping humans in the loop for high-impact decisions
Hide that AI is involved from customers
Train on all available data without privacy review
explanation: Responsible AI emphasizes transparency, bias/fairness evaluation, explainability, accountability, and HITL for high-impact decisions. The other options violate transparency, fairness, or privacy principles.

text: Which prompting technique asks the model to explain its step-by-step reasoning before giving the final answer, often improving accuracy on multi-step problems?
options: Few-shot prompting
Chain-of-thought prompting
Zero-shot prompting
Bigtable with two-region replication and read-your-writes consistency on the primary cluster while reads in the secondary region remain eventually consistent
AlloyDB Omni deployed on Compute Engine MIGs across three regions with manual switchover between primaries during a regional event
explanation: Spanner multi-region configurations provide synchronous, strongly-consistent writes across regions with RPO=0 and automatic failover, plus low local-region read latency. Cloud SQL replicas are async (RPO>0) and reads are not strongly consistent. Bigtable lacks SQL transactions and offers eventual consistency on replica clusters. AlloyDB Omni on MIGs is self-managed and not multi-master, violating the minimal-overhead requirement.

text: A team is migrating a containerized Java service that needs the JVM warm for performance and must handle long-lived WebSocket connections (up to 30 minutes). Cost should scale with traffic but absolute scale-to-zero is not required. Which compute platform is the best fit?
options: Cloud Run services with the default request-based billing and a 60-second request timeout configured per service for predictable billing
Cloud Run services configured for instance-based billing with min instances >= 1 and the WebSocket timeout raised to support long-lived connections
App Engine standard environment, which keeps instances warm and natively supports persistent bidirectional connections via WebSocket runtime config
Cloud Functions (2nd gen) configured with a 60-minute timeout to keep WebSocket sessions alive across the function invocation lifecycle
explanation: Cloud Run supports WebSockets and instance-based billing with min-instances keeps the JVM warm; per-service request timeout can be raised to 60 minutes which covers the 30-minute requirement. Default request-based billing with a 60-second timeout (option A) drops long connections. App Engine standard does not support WebSockets. Cloud Functions are not designed for long-lived bidirectional connections regardless of timeout.

text: An internal HR application running on GKE must be reachable only from corporate VPNs and from a partner SaaS over a private path, never from the public internet. Which load balancer is the right choice?
options: Global external Application Load Balancer with Cloud Armor IP allowlist restricted to the corporate VPN CIDRs and the partner SaaS egress range
Internal Application Load Balancer fronting the GKE Service, accessible from the on-prem VPN via Cloud Interconnect and from the partner via Private Service Connect
Global external proxy Network Load Balancer with Identity-Aware Proxy enforcing user-level authentication for both corporate users and the partner SaaS
Classic external Application Load Balancer with the GKE Service published via a NodePort and firewall rules restricting source IP ranges to allowed CIDRs
explanation: An internal Application Load Balancer keeps the service off the internet entirely and is reachable across Cloud Interconnect/VPN and via Private Service Connect for partner consumption. External LBs (options A, C, D) put the service on the public internet — even with allowlists, the surface is exposed and the requirement says 'never from the public internet'.

text: Which Google Cloud principal type should you grant IAM roles to when you want a Compute Engine VM to call BigQuery on its own behalf, without embedding any keys in the VM?
options: A user account belonging to the application owner
A service account attached to the VM, granted the necessary BigQuery role
A Google Group containing all engineers who maintain the VM
A Workforce Identity pool federated to the corporate IdP
explanation: Attached service accounts let the VM obtain short-lived tokens from the metadata server — no keys to manage. User accounts and groups are for human identities. Workforce Identity is for federated human users, not VM-to-API auth.

doc_links.title: Attach a service account to a VM

text: A streaming pipeline ingests clickstream events into Pub/Sub at 100k msg/s, transforms them, and lands them in BigQuery for sub-minute analytics. Which managed-service combination requires the least operational overhead?
options: A Dataproc Spark Streaming cluster reading from Pub/Sub and writing to BigQuery via the spark-bigquery connector with autoscaling enabled
A Pub/Sub BigQuery subscription writing directly to a BigQuery table when the transform is a simple schema mapping; otherwise Dataflow streaming with the Pub/Sub-to-BigQuery template
Cloud Composer DAG that pulls from Pub/Sub every minute using PythonOperator and bulk-loads JSON files into BigQuery via load jobs
Cloud Run service triggered per Pub/Sub message that issues a BigQuery streaming insert per event with at-least-once semantics
explanation: BigQuery subscriptions deliver Pub/Sub directly into BigQuery with no code when only mapping is needed; Dataflow templates handle the transform case as a fully-managed streaming runner. Dataproc Spark Streaming is more ops than required. Composer is for batch orchestration, not sub-minute streaming. A per-message Cloud Run service can work but is less efficient and harder to operate at 100k/s than the managed paths.

text: A team wants a managed CI/CD path for a Cloud Run service: build on every push to main, run tests, push the image to Artifact Registry, and deploy to a staging Cloud Run service automatically. They prefer Google-native tooling. Which combination is BEST?
options: GitHub Actions runners on a self-managed GKE cluster, building images with kaniko and using the gcloud CLI to deploy to Cloud Run on success
Cloud Build trigger on push to main running a cloudbuild.yaml that builds, tests, pushes to Artifact Registry, and deploys to Cloud Run staging — release promotion handled by Cloud Deploy
Cloud Functions triggered by an Artifact Registry Pub/Sub notification that invoke gcloud commands to deploy whichever image was last pushed to the registry path
A Compute Engine MIG of build VMs polling the Git repository every 60 seconds, building images locally, and using the Cloud Run Admin API to deploy on success
explanation: Cloud Build is the managed Google-native CI service with first-class triggers and Artifact Registry/Cloud Run integration; Cloud Deploy handles staged promotion. Self-managed GitHub runners on GKE add cluster ops; a Pub/Sub-driven Cloud Function bypasses build/test entirely; polling MIGs reinvent CI poorly.

text: A team needs to roll out a GKE Deployment as a blue/green release with the ability to instantly cut traffic back if errors spike, and they want the previous color kept warm for at least 1 hour after the switch. Which approach fits BEST?
options: Use kubectl rollout restart with maxUnavailable=0 and maxSurge=100% so the new ReplicaSet comes up before the old one is torn down, then page on errors
Use Cloud Deploy with a deployment strategy that supports a verify phase, keeping the prior release's ReplicaSet retained via Deployment revisionHistoryLimit so a rollback is one command
Switch to a Service of type LoadBalancer per color and manually update DNS A records to repoint corporate clients to the green Service after smoke tests
Configure the HorizontalPodAutoscaler to scale the old color to zero immediately on cutover so resources are reclaimed and only one color is ever running
explanation: Cloud Deploy's release strategies include verify hooks; the prior ReplicaSet is retained via revisionHistoryLimit so kubectl rollout undo or a Cloud Deploy rollback flips traffic back instantly. Restarting in place is not blue/green and has no instant rollback. DNS-based cutover has TTL-bound failover, not instant. Scaling old to zero immediately defeats the 'keep warm' requirement.

text: After a major incident, the SRE team is writing the postmortem. Which postmortem practice does Google's SRE book emphasize as MOST important?
options: Identify the engineer responsible and require remediation training so similar mistakes are not repeated by the same individual
Make the postmortem blameless — focus on systemic causes and concrete action items, not on individual fault — and share it broadly
Mark the postmortem confidential to a small leadership group so customers and auditors are not exposed to internal failure detail
Skip the postmortem if the incident lasted less than 30 minutes; reserve postmortems for outages exceeding the SLO budget for the period
explanation: Blameless postmortems and broad sharing are core SRE practices: they surface systemic causes and prevent recurrence. Naming individuals discourages truthful reporting. Hiding postmortems prevents organizational learning. Skipping based on duration loses valuable signal from near-misses.

text: Which Cloud Logging feature lets you stream log entries to BigQuery in near-real time for long-term analysis using SQL?
options: Log-based metrics with custom labels exported via the Monitoring API
A Cloud Logging sink with a BigQuery destination filtered to the desired log entries
Cloud Audit Logs Admin Activity stream, which writes directly to BigQuery by default
Error Reporting integration, which forwards grouped errors into BigQuery automatically
explanation: Logging sinks route matching log entries to destinations such as BigQuery, Cloud Storage, or Pub/Sub in near-real time. Log-based metrics produce time series, not log records. Audit Logs do not auto-export to BigQuery. Error Reporting groups errors but doesn't export to BigQuery.

text: A platform team wants every project to attribute resource cost to the owning application and environment. Engineers must be unable to spin up resources without applying the required attribution. Which combination implements this?
options: A monthly review where Finance asks teams to map BigQuery billing-export rows to applications using a spreadsheet of project-to-app mappings owned by each BU
Mandate a labels schema (app, env, owner), enforce via the constraints/compute.requireOsLogin org policy and a Cloud Function that deletes unlabeled resources nightly
Mandate a labels schema (app, env, owner), enforce required labels via Organization Policy / Resource Manager tag policies, and analyze cost via the BigQuery billing export joined on labels
Use only project-level cost separation with one project per (app, env) and a strict naming convention; skip labels entirely
explanation: Labels (or Resource Manager tags) plus the BigQuery billing export with label columns is the canonical pattern, and tag/label policies enforce attribution at create time. Monthly Finance reviews are reactive. requireOsLogin is unrelated to labels and a deletion-based enforcer is destructive. Project-per-(app,env) doesn't scale and still benefits from labels for finer slices.

text: Which prompting technique asks the model to explain its step-by-step reasoning before giving the final answer, often improving accuracy on multi-step problems?
options: Few-shot prompting
Chain-of-thought prompting
Zero-shot prompting
Role prompting
explanation: Chain-of-thought asks the model to lay out intermediate reasoning steps before the answer, improving multi-step accuracy. Few-shot supplies examples; zero-shot supplies none; role prompting sets a persona.

text: A developer wants to call the same Gemini model from a Python application, with enterprise IAM controls and integration with Vertex AI features (grounding, evaluation, monitoring). Which API surface should they use?
options: The Google AI (Gemini) API with an API key stored in Secret Manager and IP allowlists at the network egress proxy
The Vertex AI Gemini API authenticated via Application Default Credentials and the project-level IAM roles for Vertex AI
The Generative Language API via OAuth user credentials shared between developers on the team using a common refresh token
A direct HTTP call to ai.googleapis.com authenticated with a long-lived service account JSON key checked into the application repository
explanation: The Vertex AI Gemini API is the enterprise surface: ADC + IAM, VPC-SC support, and tight integration with Vertex AI grounding/evaluation/monitoring. Google AI Studio's API-key surface is for prototyping. Shared OAuth refresh tokens violate least privilege. Long-lived service account keys committed to repos are an OWASP-grade vulnerability.
